title: Observable — How a Protein Finds Its Shape
voice_suffix: clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only

shots.video_prompt: CONTINUITY LOCK @Julian-001: identical presenter face, smart-casual wardrobe, seamless neutral set, same eye-line to camera — seamless continuation of prior take, zero jump cut. CONTINUITY LOCK @Set-Seamless-Neutral-001: identical neutral grey seamless — same backdrop tone mid-grey #9A9A98, same floor sweep, same subject-to-backdrop distance, same falloff — seamless continuation, zero jump cut. STYLE LOCK @Style-Cool-Clinical-001: cool-clinical grade — neutral white balance, low saturation, accurate color reproduction, minimal stylistic grading, science and product trust, no warm cozy cast, no film grain. Presenter addresses camera on seamless neutral, crisp science hook. Camera: locked medium, direct address. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. COLOR LOCK Seamless-Neutral: backdrop #9A9A98±2%, skin true-to-life, wardrobe colors accurate, no gel spill — no magenta edge gradient, no orange spotlight cone. GRADE LOCK Cool-Clinical: grey axis neutral, skin neither pink nor yellow pushed, saturation -15% global — forbid magenta tint, forbid orange warmth, forbid teal skin on shadows, forbid vintage fade. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "A protein starts life as a floppy chain. Within milliseconds it folds into one exact three-dimensional shape — out of more possibilities than there are atoms in the universe. How does it find the right one so fast?" clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: A protein starts life as a floppy chain. Within milliseconds it folds into one exact three-dimensional shape — out of more possibilities than there are atoms in the universe. How does it find the right one so fast?
shots.on_screen: Observable · How a Protein Finds Its Shape

The chain slides downhill across an energy landscape shaped like a funnel — many different routes, all draining toward the same stable, native fold. shots.speech_text: It doesn't search them all. The chain slides downhill across an energy landscape shaped like a funnel — many different routes, all draining toward the same stable, native fold. In the crowded cell, helper proteins called chaperones give tricky chains a protected space to fold.

shots.speech_text: In nineteen seventy-three, Anfinsen showed the instructions live in the sequence itself: unfold a protein, remove the stress, and it folds right back. And the finished shapes are measured directly — hemoglobin's fold was solved by crystallography to better than two angstroms.

shots.speech_text: Get the fold right and the protein works. Get it wrong and you have the misfolding behind Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. Cracking how sequence becomes shape is one of biology's deepest problems — and we're only now learning to predict it.
